readings: Japan at War (Sources of Modern China)
Oral Histories of the Comfort Women

readingAnalyses.title: Japan at War
readingAnalyses.author: Multiple Primary Sources
readingAnalyses.source: Sources of Modern China, Chapter 17, pp. 314-333
readingAnalyses.summary: This collection presents the Pacific War through radically different perspectives: Japanese government justifications for invasion, Chinese resistance, foreign eyewitness accounts of atrocities, and Japanese press coverage that celebrated murder as sport. The contrast between Prince Konoe's rhetoric of 'peace in the Orient' and the documented reality of the Rape of Nanjing exposes the vast gulf between imperial propaganda and wartime brutality.

keyQuotes.quote: We firmly believe that it is in accordance with the right of self-defence as well as with the cause of righteousness and humanity that our country has determined to give a decisive blow to such a country, so that it may reflect upon the errors of its ways.
Little did we dream that we should see such brutality and savagery as has probably not been equalled in modern times.
One poor woman was raped thirty-seven times. Another had her five months infant deliberately smothered by the brute to stop its crying while he raped her.
The contest was 'fun,' he declared, and he thought it a good thing that both men had gone over the 100 mark without knowing that the other had done so.

thematicExamples: Prince Konoe's address (Sept 1937) frames invasion as 'self-defense' against Chinese 'anti-Japanese policies'
Ambassador Saito blames China's 'xenophobia' and suggests conditions would be 'more secure under Japanese rule'
Chiang Kai-shek's response (Dec 1938) exposes 'Greater East Asia Co-Prosperity Sphere' as euphemism for domination
Nanjing diary entries document systematic rape, murder, and looting by Japanese soldiers
Japan Advertiser newspaper celebrates 'contest to kill 100 Chinese' between two officers as sporting entertainment
realLifeConnections: Propaganda and euphemism in modern warfare — how governments justify military actions
War crimes tribunals and questions of accountability — who bears responsibility?
Japan-China relations today still shaped by contested memories of this period
Contemporary debates about teaching wartime history in Japanese textbooks

readingAnalyses.title: Oral Histories of the Comfort Women
readingAnalyses.author: Kim Tŏkchin (testimony)
readingAnalyses.source: True Stories of the Korean Comfort Women, ed. Keith Howard
readingAnalyses.summary: Kim Tŏkchin's testimony reveals the systematic sexual enslavement of Korean women during the Pacific War. Recruited at age 17 with promises of factory work, she was transported from Korea to Nagasaki to Shanghai and forced to 'serve' 30-40 soldiers daily. Her account exposes the bureaucratic apparatus of exploitation — Korean recruiters, Japanese military control, the brothel system adjacent to army units — that made such atrocities possible.

keyQuotes.quote: All I knew — all I thought I knew — was that I was going to work in a factory to earn money. I never dreamed that this could involve danger.
On the first night there I was dragged before a high-ranking soldier and raped. He had a pistol.
Each of us had to serve an average of 30 to 40 men each day, and we often had no time to sleep.
Quite a few would rush straight to penetration without condoms, saying they couldn't care less if they caught any diseases since they were likely to die on the battlefield at any moment.

thematicExamples: Korean agents recruited women for Japanese military — complicity within colonial hierarchy
Systematic deception: promises of 'factory work' to lure victims
Military organization of sexual violence — brothels adjacent to army units
Hierarchy among comfort women: Japanese vs. Korean women; officers vs. common soldiers
Silence after the war — why this history remained hidden for decades
realLifeConnections: Ongoing diplomatic tensions between Japan and South Korea over comfort women apologies and reparations
Statues of 'comfort women' as sites of memory and protest
Sexual violence in contemporary conflicts — lessons learned (or not)?
Human trafficking today uses similar deceptive recruitment tactics

overallSynthesis:
These readings confront students with the Pacific War's darkest dimensions. The Japanese government documents reveal how imperial ideology justified aggression through euphemism: 'peace in the Orient,' 'New Order in East Asia,' 'co-prosperity.' Konoe and Saito present invasion as China's fault — if only China would abandon its 'erroneous anti-Japanese policies,' there would be no need for war. This self-justifying logic was typical of imperial powers.

But the eyewitness accounts from Nanjing and Kim Tŏkchin's testimony expose what 'New Order' actually meant on the ground: systematic murder, rape, and enslavement. The anonymous diarist's matter-of-fact recording of daily atrocities — firing squads at 5 AM, women raped thirty-seven times, a five-month-old infant smothered — stands in devastating contrast to diplomatic rhetoric. The Japan Advertiser's cheerful reporting of the 'murder race' shows how thoroughly brutality was normalized.

The readings raise difficult questions about agency and complicity. Kim Tŏkchin was recruited by a Korean man; the 'comfort station' was run by a Korean couple. Japanese soldiers at Nanjing looted, raped, and murdered while their officers proved unable or unwilling to stop them. Who bears responsibility? The system or the individuals within it?

These sources also illuminate why Japan-Korea and Japan-China relations remain fraught. The wounds are still open. The 'comfort women' broke their silence only in the 1990s; debates over Japanese textbook treatments of the war continue today. History is not past.
executiveSummary:
The Pacific War (1937-1945) killed millions across Asia and left wounds that still shape regional politics today. These readings juxtapose Japanese government justifications for war with the brutal reality experienced by victims.

Japan framed its invasion as defensive — China's 'anti-Japanese' attitudes forced Japan's hand, Prince Konoe argued. The goal was 'peace in the Orient' through a 'New Order in East Asia' where Japan, Manchukuo, and China would cooperate. Chiang Kai-shek saw through this rhetoric: 'New Order' meant annexation; 'cooperation' meant slavery.

The Nanjing Massacre (Dec 1937-Jan 1938) showed what Japanese conquest actually looked like: tens of thousands murdered, women raped systematically, the city burned. Foreign observers documented atrocities their own governments could barely acknowledge. Meanwhile, Japanese newspapers celebrated officers competing to kill 100 Chinese with swords.

The 'comfort women' system institutionalized sexual slavery. Kim Tŏkchin's testimony reveals how Korean women were tricked into factory recruitment, transported across the empire, and forced to serve dozens of soldiers daily. This history remained suppressed for decades — victims were silenced by shame, governments by diplomatic convenience.

keywords.definition: Japanese wartime ideology claiming to liberate Asia from Western imperialism; in practice, a euphemism for Japanese domination
keywords.relevance: Understanding how imperial aggression was justified through anti-colonial rhetoric

keywords.definition: Mass atrocities committed by Japanese forces after capturing Nanjing (Dec 1937-Jan 1938); estimates of deaths range from tens of thousands to 300,000
keywords.relevance: Remains contentious in Japan-China relations; symbol of wartime brutality

keywords.definition: Euphemism for women — primarily Korean, Chinese, Southeast Asian — forced into sexual slavery for Japanese military
keywords.relevance: Central to Japan-Korea diplomatic tensions; questions of wartime sexual violence

keywords.definition: Japanese Prime Minister (1937-39, 1940-41) who led Japan during early Pacific War expansion
keywords.relevance: His rhetoric of 'peace' and 'self-defense' exemplifies imperial propaganda

keywords.definition: Nationalist (GMD) leader who led Chinese resistance from wartime capital Chongqing
keywords.relevance: His defiance contrasts with collaborators like Wang Jingwei

keywords.definition: Chinese politician who established Japanese puppet government in Nanjing (1940-44)
keywords.relevance: Example of collaboration; complex questions of agency under occupation

keyPassages.quote: For one country to adopt as its national policy the antagonizing of and the showing of contempt for some particular country, and to make these the underlying principles of national education by implanting such ideas in minds of the young, is unprecedented in the history of the world.
What he meant by a China reborn was that independent China was to perish and in its place an enslaved China created, which would abide by Japan's word from generation to generation.
They were the conquerors of China's capital, the seat of the hated Chiang Kai-shek government, and they were given free reign to do as they pleased.
All I knew — all I thought I knew — was that I was going to work in a factory to earn money. I never dreamed that this could involve danger.

people: Prince Konoe Fumimaro - PM who justified invasion as 'self-defense'
Chiang Kai-shek - led Chinese resistance from Chongqing
Kim Tŏkchin - Korean 'comfort woman' survivor who testified
places: Nanjing - site of 1937 massacre; Nationalist capital
Chongqing - wartime Chinese capital in interior
Shanghai - site of major 1937 battle; comfort stations
things: Co-Prosperity Sphere - euphemism for Japanese empire
Marco Polo Bridge Incident - July 7, 1937 trigger for war

keyQuestions.question: Prince Konoe claimed Japan acted in 'self-defense' and for 'righteousness and humanity.' The Nanjing diarist witnessed murder, rape, and the 'fun' of killing contests. How do we explain this gap between rhetoric and reality?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Imperial ideology creates moral frameworks that justify atrocities. Dehumanization of enemy. Distance between policymakers and soldiers. Soldiers given 'free reign' — breakdown of command or deliberate policy? Compare to other imperial atrocities.

keyQuestions.question: Kim Tŏkchin was recruited by a Korean man and managed by a Korean couple. Japanese soldiers committed the atrocities at Nanjing. Who bears responsibility — systems or individuals?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Colonial hierarchies create complicity. Korean agents worked within Japanese system — survival strategy or collaboration? Individual soldiers chose to rape and murder — but the system enabled them. Post-war tribunals struggled with these questions.

keyQuestions.question: The 'comfort women' remained silent for nearly 50 years after the war. Why did this history stay hidden, and what changed in the 1990s?
keyQuestions.talkingPoints: Shame and stigma in patriarchal societies. Governments suppressed history for diplomatic convenience (Cold War alliances). Rise of feminist movements. South Korean democratization allowed civil society to push for recognition. Compare to other delayed reckonings with historical trauma.

timeline.event: Marco Polo Bridge Incident — full-scale war begins
Chiang refuses collaboration; Wang Jingwei defects

connections.fromPrevious: Plate 19 explored Chinese nationalism through Lu Xun's critique of 'national character.' Japan's invasion galvanized exactly the nationalism Lu Xun worried about — but resistance required it.
connections.toNext: Plate 21 examines the Chinese Civil War. The war with Japan interrupted but didn't end GMD-CCP conflict; both parties emerged transformed, setting stage for Communist victory.
connections.broaderThemes: Imperial ideology and atrocity; colonial complicity; gendered violence in war; memory and reconciliation. How do societies reckon with historical trauma?

nextPreview.topic: Civil War and Revolution in China
nextPreview.preview: With Japan defeated, the unfinished civil war resumed. How did the Communists win? What role did women and land reform play in revolutionary mobilization?
readings: Women, Communism, and Revolution (He Zhen, Chen Duxiu, Mao Zedong)

teachingNotes: CONTENT WARNING: These readings contain graphic descriptions of rape, murder, and sexual slavery. Prepare students in advance.
The 'murder race' newspaper clippings are particularly disturbing — officers competing to behead 100 Chinese. Let silence sit after reading.
Kim Tŏkchin's testimony is powerful but brief. Consider having students read it aloud, slowly.
Connect to contemporary issues: Japan-Korea 'comfort women' agreement (2015) and controversy; 'comfort women' statues worldwide.
Avoid simple 'Japanese = bad' framing. Some documents show Japanese embassy officials horrified by army behavior; Korean agents recruited victims.
Ask students: What would you have done? (As a soldier? A diplomat? A Korean under colonial rule?)
The Nanjing death toll remains contested — don't get bogged down in numbers debate. Focus on documented evidence of atrocities.

locations.name: Nanjing
locations.description: Site of 1937 massacre; fell to Japan Dec 13

locations.name: Nagasaki
locations.description: Kim Tŏkchin's first stop; later atomic bombing
